title: Embeddings and Vector Search — Check your understanding

questions.question: You index your documents using OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small model. Three months later you switch to Google's text-embedding-004 to reduce costs. Users report that search results are suddenly irrelevant. What happened?
options: Google's embedding model has lower quality than OpenAI's for this use case
The new embeddings live in a different vector space — queries embedded with model B cannot meaningfully compare to documents indexed with model A
The vector database needs to be restarted after changing embedding models
text-embedding-004 produces fewer dimensions, which reduces similarity accuracy
questions.explanation: Each embedding model defines its own vector space. Similarity scores between vectors only have meaning when both vectors come from the same model. Mixing models — old documents embedded with model A, new queries embedded with model B — produces meaningless scores. The fix is to re-embed and re-index all documents with the new model before switching queries to it.

questions.question: You want to add semantic search to a RAG system that uses Claude as the generation model. Which embedding provider should you use?
options: Anthropic's embedding API — it's designed to work best with Claude
Any embedding provider — OpenAI, Google, Cohere, or an open-source model; Anthropic does not offer an embedding model
You must use the same provider for embeddings and generation for the vectors to be compatible
OpenAI only — other providers' embeddings are not compatible with most vector databases
questions.explanation: Anthropic does not offer an embedding model. For RAG systems that generate with Claude, you pick an embedding model from another provider (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small, Google text-embedding-004, Cohere embed-english-v3.0, or open-source models like all-MiniLM-L6-v2). The generation model and embedding model are completely independent — you can mix any combination.

questions.question: Why is cosine similarity preferred over Euclidean distance for most text embedding use cases?
options: Cosine similarity is faster to compute than Euclidean distance
Cosine similarity is invariant to vector magnitude, so a short and long document on the same topic score similarly
Vector databases only support cosine similarity
Euclidean distance cannot handle negative numbers in embedding vectors
questions.explanation: Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors, ignoring their magnitude (length). This matters for text because a short and a long document on the same topic will have vectors pointing in the same direction but at different magnitudes. Euclidean distance would penalise the longer document. For semantic meaning, direction matters more than magnitude, making cosine the standard choice for text embeddings.

questions.question: Your knowledge base has 5 million documents. Exact nearest-neighbour search is too slow for interactive use. What technique do production vector databases use to solve this?
options: They reduce all embeddings to 2 dimensions before searching
They only search a random sample of vectors on each query
Approximate Nearest Neighbour (ANN) algorithms like HNSW trade a small accuracy loss for large speed gains by searching a structured index rather than every vector
They cache the top-100 results for common queries and skip search for those
questions.explanation: Exact nearest-neighbour search is O(n) — it compares the query to every stored vector, which doesn't scale to millions of documents at interactive latency. ANN algorithms like HNSW build a graph or cluster structure over the vectors and navigate it to find approximate nearest neighbours, checking a fraction of the corpus per query. The accuracy loss (some true neighbours may be missed) is small and usually acceptable for retrieval tasks.

questions.question: You are indexing 10,000 documents. What is wrong with this approach: `for doc in documents: embed(doc)` with a single API call per document?
options: Embedding APIs don't support indexing more than 1,000 documents
It is significantly slower and more expensive than batch embedding — all major embedding APIs accept a list of texts and return a list of vectors in one call
Individual embedding calls produce lower-quality vectors than batch calls
The vector database cannot accept embeddings from individual API calls
questions.explanation: All major embedding APIs support batch input: pass a list of texts, get a list of vectors back. Embedding one document at a time means 10,000 individual HTTP requests — each with network latency, rate limit exposure, and per-call overhead. Batching reduces this to tens or hundreds of requests. For 10,000 documents, the difference can be minutes vs seconds of indexing time and meaningfully lower API costs.
